coredata.dc:title: Boosting the electricity generation of nonclassical electroactive microorganisms enabled by carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Siyu
Zhao, Xinpeng
Guo, Xinqi
Zhang, Gaoqi
Li, Tianchong
Xu, Shiyu
Zhao, Yufu
Li, Jian
Liu, Shouxin
Chen, Zhijun
Li, Shujun
Yang, Chenhui
coredata.dc:description: Producing electricity via microorganisms is promising as a sustainable and inexpensive energy source. However, most of the reported classical electroactive microorganisms are unusual in the environment, hindering their practical application. Here, we used carbon dots (CDs) to boost the power generation of common but nonclassical electroactive microorganisms, such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, in the natural environment. The CDs, which have good biocompatibility and high electron transfer efficiency, are taken up by bacterial cells and enable electrons to migrate rapidly to the ambient electrode because of the formation of robust electron transfer networks. The CDs promote electrochemical redox activity and increase the internal charge generation, and reduce the electrochemical impedance of the bacteria. As a result, the CDs facilitate rapid and high power generation in the microbial chemical cells and microbial fuel cells, with a nearly 94-fold and 172-fold improvement in current and power output compared with unmodified Escherichia coli. This work offers an important strategy for enhancing the generation of bioelectricity and facilitates the functional optimization of technologies that depend on microbial electron transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microbial fuel cells
Power generation
Carbon dots
Nonclassical electroactive microorganisms
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of the CDs
Assessment of the biocompatibility of CDs
Bacterial protein assay by modified Bradford protein assay
Morphology, structure and composition of CDs
Internalization of CDs by bacterial cells.
Enhanced bioelectricity production by bacteria functionalized with CDs.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022BB05), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral (LBH-Z21033) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890774, 31870553).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the route to synthesize CDs and illustration of enhanced bioelectricity generation by Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Rhodopseudomonas palustris (R. palustris) functionalized using CDs.

Morphology and composition of the CDs. (a) TEM image, (b) high-resolution TEM image, (c) XRD pattern, (d) Raman spectrum, (e) FT-IR spectrum, (f) XPS survey spectrum, and (g-i) C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of CDs.

Viability of bacteria-CDs. Growth curves of (a) E. coli, (b) S. aureus, and (c) R. palustris with increasing concentrations of CDs (0–500 μg mL−1) (n = 8, Data represent mean ± standard deviation, ‘n’ represents the number of biological repeats). CLSM images stained by calcein-AM and PI of (d) and (g) E. coli, (e) and (h) S. aureus, and (f) and (i) R. palustris with/without the addition of CDs (100 μg mL−1), where the excitation and emission wavelengths are 490 and 515 nm for calcein-AM and 535 and 617 nm for PI.

Distribution of CDs in bacteria. TEM images of cross-section slices of (a), (b) E. coli incubated with CDs and (c) untreated E. coli; (d), (e) S. aureus incubated with CDs and (f) untreated S. aureus; (g), (h) R. palustris incubated with CDs and (i) untreated R. palustris. Where the red cycles at different zones of cells are assigned to the aggregates.

Enhanced bioelectricity generation by E. coli-CDs. (a) MEC current output vs time curves (n = 3). Data in (a) represent the mean ± standard deviation, and ‘n’ represents the number of biological repeats. (b) MFC Voltage output curves over 3 cycles. (c) and (d) Curves of voltage and power density vs current density for E. coli-CDs and unmodified E. coli. (e) and (f) LED powered by MFCs constructed from E. coli-CDs. The concentration of CDs in a-c and e, f is 100 μg mL−1.

Increased electron transfer activity by bacteria-CDs. (a) CV (the scanning speed is 0.01 V s−1, and the electrolyte is PBS (50 mmol/L)), (b) EIS, and (c) ETSA of MECs constructed using E. coli-CDs and unmodified E. coli. Data in (c) represent the mean ± standard deviation, ‘n’ in c = 4, representing the number of biological repeats, two-tailed Student’s t test: *p < 0.001. The concentration of CDs in all MECs and MFCs was 100 μg mL−1. (d) Illustration showing strengthening of the electron transfer network inside nonclassical electroactive microorganisms by CDs.

head.ce:title: Boosting the electricity generation of nonclassical electroactive microorganisms enabled by carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Quality Inspection and Research in Heilongjiang Province, Harbin 150028, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Quality Inspection and Research in Heilongjiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Quality Inspection and Research in Heilongjiang Province, Harbin 150028, PR China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the route to synthesize CDs and illustration of enhanced electron transfer by CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Producing electricity via microorganisms is promising as a sustainable and inexpensive energy source. However, most of the reported classical electroactive microorganisms are unusual in the environment, hindering their practical application. Here, we used carbon dots (CDs) to boost the power generation of common but nonclassical electroactive microorganisms, such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, in the natural environment. The CDs, which have good biocompatibility and high electron transfer efficiency, are taken up by bacterial cells and enable electrons to migrate rapidly to the ambient electrode because of the formation of robust electron transfer networks. The CDs promote electrochemical redox activity and increase the internal charge generation, and reduce the electrochemical impedance of the bacteria. As a result, the CDs facilitate rapid and high power generation in the microbial chemical cells and microbial fuel cells, with a nearly 94-fold and 172-fold improvement in current and power output compared with unmodified Escherichia coli. This work offers an important strategy for enhancing the generation of bioelectricity and facilitates the functional optimization of technologies that depend on microbial electron transfer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioelectricity, generated by the bioelectrochemical process of microorganisms, exhibits great potential in power generation, bioremediation of water pollutants, and bioelectrosynthesis [1–6]. However, bioelectricity generation is low due to the barrier of cellular nonconductive components and sluggish transmembrane and extracellular electron transfer (EET) processes [7]. To overcome the low efficiency of bioelectricity generation, three main strategies have been employed [8,9]. The first approach is to modify the electrode to either increase bacterial adsorption or conductivity, which is achieved by modifying the electrode with carbon materials, conductive polymers, nanoparticles, chelate complexes, nanomaterials and polypyrrole nanotube membrane [10–18]. Another approach is to promote the protein expression of the metal respiratory pathway or the secretion of flavin molecules via genetic engineering [8,19,20]. The third approach is to directly modify the microorganisms with nanomaterials, polymers or conjugated oligoelectrolytes. To date, metal nanoparticles (Pd, Au and FeS2), conductive polymers, e.g., polypyrrole and carbon dots (CDs), and conjugated oligoelectrolytes have been employed [4,13,21–23]. Among all these materials, enhancing bioelectricity generation via CDs has received particular attention since CDs exhibit superior physicochemical and unique electronic properties, excellent biocompatibility and stability, low preparation cost and high electron transfer efficiency [9,22,24–26]. EET processes are greatly enhanced through these three strategies, eventually triggering more bioelectricity generation [27].

Nevertheless, most of these studies focused on classical Shewanella and Geobacter spp [28–30]. These microorganisms live in the deep underground or the sea, indicating that they cannot be employed in practical conditions [31–34]. To this end, attention is given to these nonclassical electroactive but common microorganisms in the natural environment, such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. However, these common microorganisms only produce trivial amounts of bioelectricity since they are deficient in cytochromes, the membrane-associated and iron-containing heme protein needed for EET channel formation, in comparison to their classical counterpart [35,36].

Here, we describe a simple and far-reaching strategy that exploits a biotic-abiotic system constructed using CDs to boost the bioelectricity generation of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris, which are not typically regarded as electroactive microorganisms (Fig. 1 ). The CDs, which were prepared from xylose and dopamine hydrochloride using a hydrothermal method, have good biocompatibility and high electron transfer efficiency. Integration of the CDs into microorganisms led to the formation of robust electron transfer networks. Such biotic-abiotic systems effectively reduce electrochemical impedance, increase electron transfer and generate more charge. Microbial electrochemical cells (MECs) and microbial fuel cells (MFCs) assembled from bacteria functionalized using CDs (bacteria-CDs) can generate as much as nearly 100-fold more power output than systems assembled from unmodified bacteria. From the viewpoint of system design, we have devised a simple and general method for boosting power generation by constructing a biotic-abiotic system using CDs.

ce:list-item.ce:label: D-
ce:list-item.ce:para: (+)-Xylose, and dopamine hydrochloride used for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Aladdin Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. Luria-Bertani broth (LB), tryptone soy broth (TSB) and sodium lactate used for culturing Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC25312), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC29213) and Rhodopseudomonas palustris (R. palustris, BNCC232041) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China), Qingdao Haibo Biological Co., ltd. (Qingdao, China) and Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd., respectively. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50 mmol/L) containing Na2HPO4·12H2O (29 mmol/L), Na2HPO4·2H2O (21 mmol/L), NH4Cl (6 mmol/L) and KCl (2 mmol/L) used in the assessment of MECs and MFCs were purchased from Aladdin Co., ltd. Carbon cloth (WOS 1011) used as the anode in the MECs and MFCs was purchased from Taiwan Carbon Energy Technology Co., ltd. (Taiwan China). A modified Bradford protein assay kit used for protein measurements was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-HCl, sodium sulfite (Aladdin Co., ltd.), and 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd.) were used in the assay to measure electron transport system activity (ETSA). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL ltd., Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using an X’Pert Pro materials powder diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands) over the range of 10-80°. The structure and composition of the CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectra was recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution confocal Raman microscope (Horiba, ltd., Kyoto, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical reaction, CDs were synthesized by heating a solution of xylose (200 mg) and dopamine hydrochloride (189 mg) in deionized water (15 mL) at 180 °C for 8 h in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL capacity). After cooling, the resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filtration membrane and then dialyzed against water for 3 days using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. The purified solution was then concentrated by rotary evaporation to provide xylose-based CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the biocompatibility of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli and S. aureus were cultivated in Luria-Bertani broth at 37 °C with an oscillation frequency of 150 rpm. R. palustris was cultivated in tryptone soy broth at 30 °C with an oscillation frequency of 180 rpm. The growth curves of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris in the presence of CDs (0–500 μg mL−1) were measured by recording the absorbance of the bacterial solutions at 600 nm using a microplate reader.

After incubation with the CDs for 6 h, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm and then dispersed in PBS (50 mmol/L, 5 mL). Calcein-AM (2 μmol mL−1) and propidium iodide (PI) (4.5 μmol mL−1) were added to the bacterial solutions in PBS, which were then cultivated in the dark at 37 °C for 15 min. The stained bacterial cells were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope. Cell viability following cultivation with the CDs (0–500 μg mL−1) was determined using an MTT assay [37].

ETSA was measured to determine the extent of intracellular charge generation by the three different types of bacteria-CDs [22]. Specifically, after 6 h of cocultivation with the CDs in MECs, the bacterial solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min with Tris-HCl buffer (1 mL), aqueous sodium sulfite (0.36 %, 0.5 mL) and 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride solution (0.4 %, 1 mL). Formaldehyde (∼37 wt% in water, 5 mL) was added to the MECs to terminate the reaction. After incubation at 37 °C for 10 min, the bacterial solutions (10 mL) were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatants were removed. Acetone (3 mL) was then added as the extractant, and the mixtures were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The absorbance of the supernatants at 485 nm was then measured using a UV–Vis absorption spectrometer. The ETSA was calculated using the following formula:
ESTA =
O
D 485
× V
kwt

In this formula, OD485 represents the absorbance value at 485 nm, V represents the volume of acetone (mL), k represents the slope of the ETSA standard curve, w represents the protein weight (μg mL−1), and t represents the coculture time in MECs (h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial protein assay by modified Bradford protein assay
ce:section.ce:para: First, 12 wells on the ELISA plate were selected as the standard group, and each standard sample was set with a duplicate group. Then, 20 μL BSA standard protein solution of corresponding concentration was added to each well. The experimental group was also set with two duplicate groups. 20 μL bacterial solution was added to each well, 200 μL of Bradford working solution was added to each well, and the solutions were quickly mixed. After 5 min of reaction at room temperature (25–30 °C), the absorbance value at 595 nm of each well was measured on a microplate reader. Then, the average OD595 of each well in the standard group was set as the ordinate, and the corresponding protein concentration was the abscissa. A standard curve was drawn in Microsoft Excel, and then the protein concentration of the sample solution was calculated by the standard curve.

Three-electrode electrochemical cells (42 mL) were used to investigate the effect of the CDs on the generation of bioelectricity. The working electrode was a 1 × 2 cm2 carbon cloth connected by titanium wire, and the reference electrode and counter electrode were silver/silver chloride saturated electrodes. Solutions of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris in PBS (OD600 = 0.6, 1 mL) with sodium acetate (5 mmol/L) in PBS (50 mmol/L) were used as the electrolyte. Current output vs time curves were measured using a CHI1040C 8 channel potentiostat (CH Instruments, ltd., China). After substrate exhaustion, analysis by cyclic voltammetry (CV) over the potential range −0.60 to 0.40 V was conducted, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., ltd., Shanghai, China).

Two compartment MFCs, with capacities of 120 mL for both cathode and anode chambers, were used to test power generation. Carbon cloth (2 × 4 cm2) was used as the electrode in each chamber, and the electrodes were connected via a constant resistance (1 kΩ) titanium wire (Φ, 0.5 mm). A proton exchange membrane (CMI-7000S) was used to separate the cathode and anode chambers. Solutions of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris in PBS (OD600 = 0.6, 3 mL) with sodium acetate (5 mmol/L) in PBS (50 mmol/L) were used as the anolytes, and potassium ferricyanide (50 mmol/L) in PBS (50 mmol/L) was used as the catholyte. The voltage and power output of the MFCs were recorded using a DAQ65410 data acquisition system (Tektronix, Inc., Beaverton, OR, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and composition of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images show that the CDs have a relatively uniform size distribution with an average diameter of ∼2.8 nm (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1) and an average height of ∼2.0 nm (Fig. S2). The high-resolution TEM image of the CDs (Fig. 2b) shows a lattice distance of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the in-plane spacing of (100) grapheme [38,39]. As with most previously reported CDs, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs (Fig. 2c) shows an obvious broad peak at 2θ of ∼24°, which corresponds to the interlayer spacing of graphene. The Raman spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2d) shows two peaks at 1302 and 1552 cm−1, with an IG/ID ratio of ∼3.6, suggesting that the proportion of crystalline graphitization is higher than that of disordered carbon structures [40–42]. Both the XRD and Raman spectra thus indicate that the CDs have a relatively high degree of graphitization and crystallinity.

The composition and structure of the CDs were further investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows a broad characteristic peak at 3350–3000 cm−1 attributed to the C-OH/NH stretching vibration, a peak at 2960 cm−1 corresponding to the CH stretching vibration of aromatic structures, a peak at 1710 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching vibration, prominent peaks at 1610, 1500 and 1290 cm−1 corresponding to the CC, NH and CN stretching vibrations, respectively, and an obvious peak at 1030 cm−1 corresponding to the COC bending vibration [22,43,44]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows that the CDs are composed of C, N and O species with an atomic ratio of 1.00:0.29:0.09 (Table S1). The C 1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s XPS spectrum shows three binding energy peaks, graphitic N at 401.7 eV, C-NH2 at 400.4 eV and amino N at 399.2 eV [45–47]. The O 1s XPS spectrum shows two obvious peaks at 531.5 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to CO and CO bonds, respectively. These results show that the CDs contain C, N and O species and confirm the presence of N- and O-related surface functional groups, e.g., –OH, –CO and –NH2.

The biotoxicity and biocompatibility of the CDs were assessed by measuring the growth curves of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris in the presence of increasing concentrations of CDs (0–500 μg mL−1). In each assessment, absorbance at 600 nm was determined. After coincubation with CDs at concentrations as high as 500 μg mL−1 for over 100 h, all three types of bacterial cells maintained high viability (Fig. 3 a-c), indicating that the CDs do not inhibit the growth or reproduction of any of the bacteria. In an MTT assay using human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), the survival rate of the cells in the presence of CDs (0–400 μg mL−1) was >80 % (Fig. S3). The proportion of viable/dead cells after coincubation with CDs was assessed by examining the cells using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) following staining with calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI). Calcein-AM is highly lipophilic and cell membrane-permeable. Although calcein-AM itself is not fluorescent, the calcein generated from calcein-AM by esterases in viable cells emits strong green fluorescence. Calcein-AM thus stains only living cells. In contrast, the nuclear stain PI cannot pass through the cell membrane of living cells but can penetrate disordered areas of the membrane of dead cells to reach the nucleus. PI thus stains only dead cells [48]. Bacterial cells killed by treatment with 70 % alcohol for 30 min were used as a control, and in this case, almost all the cells were stained by PI (Fig. S4). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs showed blue to green emission (Fig. S5), while the CLSM images of bacteria incubated only with CDs did not show an obvious fluorescence signal (Fig. S6). The PL quenching of CDs is possibly due to the binding and charge transfer of the CDs with the intracellular components [22]. This result confirms that CDs do not affect the viable/dead cell test. CLSM images of bacterial cells stained by calcein-AM and PI in the absence and presence of CDs (100 μg mL−1) showed distinct green fluorescence (calcein-AM), with very little red fluorescence (PI) (Fig. 3d-i and Fig. S7), indicating a high proportion of viable cells. The growth curves, MTT assay and viable/dead cell staining assay all clearly indicate that the CDs have good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization of CDs by bacterial cells.
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the CDs on the bacteria was investigated by observing the surface morphology of the cells by TEM [49–51]. Following incubation with CDs, large black spots were visible on the surfaces of E. coli, in sharp contrast to the surfaces of untreated bacteria, which were relatively smooth (Fig. S8). The intracellular distribution of the CDs, as well as their uptake by the bacterial cells, was investigated via TEM using cross-sectional slices of the cells. Following incubation with CDs, the cellular cross-sectional TEM images of E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris all showed obvious black aggregates, with a typical diameter of ∼8–40 nm, which is much bigger than that of CDs (∼2.8 nm) (Fig. 4 a, b, d, e, g and h). These results indicate that the CDs are internalized by bacterial cells and located on the surface and inside the cells. It is the surface functional groups of the –OH, –CO and –NH2 of CDs that allow the combination between CDs and cellular components. It is reasonable to infer that these augmented black aggregates originate from the combination of CDs with components inside the bacteria, e.g., cytochromes/polypeptide/enzyme possibly related to the electron transfer, respiratory and substrate oxidation [52–55]. Unfortunately, it is difficult to clarify the specific binding between CDs and protein because of the complexity and multicomponent nature of bacterial cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced bioelectricity production by bacteria functionalized with CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: In proof-of-concept experiments, MECs and MFCs were used to explore the effect of CDs on power generation by these bacteria. As shown in Fig. 5 a, because of the nonconductive component of unmodified E. coli, the peak current output of MECs was only ∼0.3 ± 0.0 μA after 52 h. In contrast, the peak current output of MECs constructed using E. coli-CDs was 28.6 ± 3.7 μA after 6 h, a 94-fold increase compared with that of MECs constructed using unmodified bacteria. As shown in Fig. S9, MECs constructed using two additional modified bacteria, S. aureus-CDs and R. palustris-CDs, also showed significant enhancement of current output compared with that of unmodified bacteria (43.0 ± 1.5 μA vs 0.8 ± 0.1 μA for S. aureus and 33.0 ± 1.6 μA vs 0.4 ± 0.1 μA for R. palustris, representing 53- and 82-fold increases, respectively). Following the experiments, the total amount of coulomb electrons collected by each MEC was calculated [56]. The integrated charge in MECs constructed using E. coli-CDs was 3.1C, which was 30-fold higher than that of MECs constructed using unmodified E. coli (Fig. S10a). Analogously, the integrated charge originating from S. aureus-CDs and R. palustris-CDs was much higher than that originating from unmodified bacteria. (Fig. S10b and c).

The voltage of a dual-chamber MFC with 1.0 kΩ external resistance was recorded using a multichannel potentiometer to evaluate the enhanced performance of bacteria-CDs. The cell voltage was measured as a function of time in MFCs constructed from E. coli and E. coli-CDs over three cycles. During each MFC period, bacteria are reproduced, the biofilm is constantly renewed, and the electrons generated from substrate oxidation (i.e., acetate) are transferred through the extracellular electron transfer chain to extracellular electron acceptors [57]. The MFC voltage drops to the minimum until the substrate is exhausted. The voltage output of the MFCs constructed using E. coli-CDs showed a rapid rise within 4 h, reaching a maximum voltage of ∼135.9 mV whereas the voltage output of MFCs constructed using unmodified E. coli increased rather slowly, with a maximum voltage of only ∼2.7 mV (Fig. 5b). The voltage outputs of MFCs constructed using S. aureus-CDs and R. palustris-CDs were also distinctly higher than those from MFCs constructed from unmodified bacteria (Fig. S11). Interestingly, faster increases in current and voltage were observed in MECs and MFCs constructed using bacteria-CDs, possibly suggesting faster substrate oxidation and implying higher metabolic rates. Polarization and power density curves were also measured in the MFCs. The maximum power densities in MFCs constructed using E. coli-CDs, S. aureus-CDs and R. palustris-CDs were 13.87, 7.17 and 10.96 mW m−2, respectively. These values are 172-, 89- and 218-fold higher than those of MFCs constructed using unmodified E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris (0.08, 0.08 and 0.05 mW m−2, respectively) (Fig. 5c, d and S12). As a real-life application, we constructed a light-emitting diode (LED) of the pattern “NEFU”, assembled using 35 mini bulbs. This LED emitted bright light when powered by MFCs constructed from E. coli-CDs (Fig. 5e and f).

Data obtained using both MECs and MFCs indicate that CDs can efficiently boost power generation by microorganisms such as E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris that are not typically considered to be electroactive.

CV curves were constructed to study the electrochemical activity of the bacteria-CD systems. The CV curve of E. coli-CDs showed a substantially increased current signal compared with that of unmodified E. coli (Fig. 6 a). Since the CDs and carbon cloth alone produced no obvious redox peak signal (Fig. S13), the electrochemical activity of the MECs must originate from the modified bacteria. Both the enhanced reduction peak and oxidation peak currents can therefore be attributed to increased electron transfer by the combination of CDs and catalytically active bacterial proteins [19,58].

We next evaluated the ability of CDs to boost electron transport capacity using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [59,60]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the semicircle diameter (Rct) of the E. coli-CDs was smaller than that of unmodified E. coli, indicating that the CDs facilitate electron transfer, thus improving the electrical conductivity of the bacteria-CD systems.

An electron transport system activity (ETSA) assay was carried out to investigate the effect of CDs on electron transfer within the bacteria and determine the increase in intracellular charge generation by functionalization with CDs (Fig. 6c) [22]. ETSA in E. coli-CDs was substantially higher than that in unmodified E. coli (22.7 ± 0.8 μg mg−1h−1 vs 7.4 ± 0.4 μg mg−1h−1), suggesting greater charge generation in E. coli-CDs.

Similar enhancements in the electrochemical activity of S. aureus-CDs and R. palustris-CDs were revealed by the CV curves, EIS spectra and ETSA measurements (Figs. S15–17). CDs thus significantly enhance electrochemical activity, reduce electrochemical impedance and increase intracellular charge generation in both S. aureus and R. palustris.

We next derived an experimentally inferred estimate of the effect of CDs on electron transfer in the abiotic-biological system (Fig. 6d). CDs contribute to the construction of more efficient electron transfer channels through the cellular cytoplasm within the bacteria, including the inner and outer membranes. This increases transmembrane and extracellular electron transfer and thus significantly improves the power generation capacity.

In this work, hybrid biotic-abiotic systems were constructed by integrating engineered CDs into nonclassical electroactive microorganisms, i.e., E. coli, S. aureus and R. palustris, to boost power generation. Notably, compared to the classic electroactive bacterial for energy generation, such as, Shewanella and Geobacter spp, as-used bacterial was more common in the daily environment and could be used practically in the future.

The nitrogen-doped xylose-based CDs have relatively high graphitization and crystallinity and thus efficiently promote electron transfer. After functionalization of the bacteria by the CDs, relatively unobstructed electron transport channels, which enhance electrochemical activity, reduce electrochemical impedance and increase intracellular charge generation, are established inside the bacteria. The generation of bioelectricity is distinctly elevated, with almost 94-fold and 172-fold improvements in current and power output, respectively.

The contributions of the xylose-based CDs can be summarized as follows:
a)
Good biocompatibility of the CDs allows cellular uptake and interaction with intracellular components to build more efficient transmembrane and extracellular electron transfer networks.
b)
A high degree of graphitization in the carbon core of the CDs enables high electron transfer capacity, which improves electron transfer from the cytoplasmic membrane across the periplasm and outer membrane to the extracellular medium.
c)
More rapid electron transfer by the CDs increases the redox reaction and thus promotes metabolic activities, i.e., increased intracellular charge and power generation.

The importance of this work lies in the design of more efficient electron transfer pathways for bacteria that are not traditionally regarded as electrochemically active. These more efficient pathways will significantly improve power generation in real-life applications. In the future, this strategy will be applied to biofabrication, i.e., the production of hydrogen, ethanol and acetic acid, as well as to other microbial electron transfer-related technologies. In addition, a comprehensive investigation on the proposed binding between CDs and cellular protein would be clarified for benefiting the understanding of the molecular biology aspects.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022BB05), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral (LBH-Z21033) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890774, 31870553).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142147.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
